m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
void square (int *x) {
      *x = (*x + 1) * (*x);
}
int main ( ) {
int num = 10;
square(&num);
           cout << num;
           return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
In above code num variable is passed by address. So when  *x is changing value of num it will reflect in main.We call function by passing address of num variable so *x is nothig but value 10.
so value became (10+1)*10 that is 110.

It will return value to its caller
Maybe or maynot return any value to its caller

B. Option B is incorrect because here return type is not mention .void is in argument list.
C. We can't say anything about return because nothis is mention about return type.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
void Sum (int a, int b, int & c) {
    a = b + c;
    b = a + c;
    c = a + b;
}
int main()
{
     int x = 2, y =3;
     Sum(x, y, y);
     cout << x << ' ' << y;
     return 0;
 }
m_Item1.Explanation:
void Sum(int a, int b, int & c)
{
//  here a is value of x ,b is value of y, and c is reference of y
    a = b + c;     a is 6  because  3+3
    b = a + c;     b is 9   because 6+3
    c = a + b;     c is 15 because 6+9
//  so a became 6  and c became 15
but c is reference of y so when we change, so value get reflected to main

}

m_Item1.Explanation: Class is user defined type, which is collection of data members and member functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int arr[2][3][2]={{{2,4},{7,8},{32,4},}, {{2,42},{2,34},{9,4}, }};
    cout<<*((*((*(arr+1))+2))+0)<<" "<<arr[0][1][1];
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
It is 3D Array which has 2 depths,3,rows and 2 columns.
*((*((*(arr+1))+2))+0)  means we want to access element from depth with index 1, row with index 2 and column with index 0, which is 9.
arr[0][1][1] means we want to access element from depth 0, row 1 and column 1 which is 8

What is the output of this program?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
void copy (int a, int& b, int& c)
{
      a *= 2;
      b *= 2;
      c *= 2;
}
int main ()
{
       int x = 1, y = 3, z = 7;
       copy (x, y, z);
       cout << "x =" << x << " y ="<< y << " z =" << z;
       return 0;
 }
m_Item1.Explanation: Reference is just new name or nick name to original variable so whatever changes we are doing with reference it will automatically reflect in original,as the copy function is called by reference the value of y and z get modified in copy function hence output is 1, 6 and 14.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
template<typename type>
class Test {
        public:
        Test()   {    };
        ~Test()    {     };
        type Funct1(type Var1)   {
            return Var1;
        }
        type Funct2(type Var2)   {
            return Var2;
        }
};
int main() {
        Test<int> Var1;
        Test<float> Var2;
        cout << Var1.Funct1(200) << endl;
        cout << Var2.Funct2(3.123) << endl;
        return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: In Above code Funct1 is called which will return 200 value , and we are printing it's value 200  and next time  Funct2 is called which will return 3.12 values, and we are printing it's value that is 3.13

m_Item1.QuestionText: '::'  can be used as an unary operator in C++

Options.OptionText: Can be used in C but not in C++

m_Item1.Explanation:
The scope resolution operator can be used as both unary and binary. You can use the unary scope resolution operator if a namespace scope or global scope name is hidden by a particular declaration of an equivalent name during a block or class.
Example:
if you have a global variable of name my_var and a local variable of name my_var, to access global my_var, you'll need to use the scope resolution operator i.e.    ::my_var

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can we overload postfix version of operator++?

Options.OptionText: void operator++(int, int) { }

m_Item1.Explanation: Overoading postfix operator require special input parameter int ,to distinguish between prefix and postfix operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class ABC
{};
void  main() {
    ABC *ob = 0;
    delete ob;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
ABC *ob = 0; declares a pointer ob of type ABC* and initializes it to nullptr (or 0 in older C++ syntax, which is equivalent to nullptr).
delete ob; attempts to delete the pointer ob.
In C++, it is safe to call delete on a nullptr. The delete operator checks if the pointer is nullptr before attempting to free memory. If the pointer is nullptr, delete does nothing and returns safely.
Therefore, the code will compile and run without any errors or issues.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output if the input provided is 5?
#include < iostream>
using namespace std;
int a = 1000;
class Test
{
   int a;
   public:
  Test()
  {
     a = 0;
  }
  void get_a()
 {
    int a;
    cout << “Enter Value of A :”;
    cin >> a;
  }
  void put_a()
  {
     cout << endl << a << “ ” << ::a;
  }
};
void main()
{
   Test t1;
   t1.get_a();
   t1.put_a();
}
m_Item1.Explanation: get_a function has local member a  value is not assign to class member a. So put_a() function will print a which is class members values which is assign be default constructor that is 0. and ::a will print value which is globle value 1000

A reference must always be initialized outside all functions.

cin skips all whitespace (spaces, tabs, new lines, etc.) by default. You can either change its behavior, or use a slightly different mechanism. To change its behavior, use the manipulator noskipws, as follows:

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following keywords do you think can be used when declaring static members in a class?
(i) Public
(ii) Private
(iii) Protected
m_Item1.Explanation: Access specifiers will not give you error while defining the static member. Access specifiers control the access of the member variables.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In case of a copy constructor, which of the following is true?

Options.OptionText: Used to instantiate an object from another existing object
To copy one object to another existing object.
Can be a substitute for a ‘=’ operator.

m_Item1.Explanation:
The copy constructor is a constructor which creates an object by initializing it with an object of the same class, which has been created previously. The copy constructor is used to:
Initialize one object from another of the same type.
Copy an object to pass it as an argument to a function.
Copy an object to return it from a function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
void execute(int x, int y = 200) {
  int temp = x + y;
  x += temp;
  if (y != 200)
    cout << temp << "  " << x << "  " << y << "  " << endl;
}
int main() {
  int a = 50, b = 20;
  execute(b);
  cout << a << "  " << b << "  " << endl;
  execute(a, b);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
1. in first fucntion call b variable is pass so second variable is from default parameter so call is execute(20,200) where we are not returnring anything and condition in fucntion is also false so no change in values.
2. In second call two values are passed so call is as execute(a,b) so condition is satisfy and it will print value of temp,x and y.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which header file is needed to use 'cin' and 'cout' in a C++ program?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'cin' and 'cout' are part of the iostream library, so you need to include <iostream>.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the 'cin' object in C++?

Options.OptionText: To output text to the console
To read input from the standard input device

m_Item1.Explanation: 'cin' is used to read data input by the user from the standard input device (usually the keyboard).

m_Item1.QuestionText: What operator is used with 'cin' to stream data into variables?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '>>' operator is used with 'cin' to extract data from the input stream and store it in variables.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly uses 'cout' to display 'Hello, World!'?
m_Item1.Explanation: String literals in C++ should be enclosed in double quotes, and '<<' is the correct operator to insert data into the output stream using 'cout'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If 'int a;' is declared and you perform 'cin >> a;' but input is 'abc', what happens?

Options.OptionText: a is assigned the ASCII value of 'a'
Input operation fails, and a retains its previous value

m_Item1.Explanation: When non-numeric data is input for an integer variable, the input operation fails, and the variable retains its prior value. Additionally, the 'cin' object might be in an error state.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the result of the following code? 'cout << 5 + 10 << endl;'
m_Item1.Explanation: The expression '5 + 10' evaluates to '15', which is then sent to the output stream using 'cout'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which flag can be used with 'cout' to format output as fixed-point notation?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'std::fixed' flag is used to format numerical output as fixed-point notation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What kind of operator is '<<' in the context of using 'cout'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '<<' operator is known as the stream insertion operator, used to insert data into the output stream with 'cout'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you chain multiple outputs in a single 'cout' statement?

Options.OptionText: By using the '<<' operator between them
Not possible with a single 'cout' statement

m_Item1.Explanation: You can chain multiple outputs in a single 'cout' statement by using '<<' between each item you want to output.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which object can be used along with 'cout' to insert a newline after the output?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'std::endl' manipulator is used with 'cout' to insert a newline and flush the stream.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a default parameter in C++?

Options.OptionText: A parameter that must be provided by the user each time a function is called.
A parameter that has a predefined value and can be omitted when calling a function.
A parameter that changes its value every time the function is called.
A parameter that is used only in constructors.

m_Item1.Explanation: A default parameter in C++ is a function parameter that is given a default value and does not need to be explicitly passed by the caller.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where should default parameters be specified in a function definition?

Options.OptionText: At the beginning of the parameter list.
In the middle of the parameter list.
At the end of the parameter list.
Default parameters can be specified anywhere in the parameter list.

m_Item1.Explanation: Default parameters must be specified at the end of the parameter list because once a default value is defined for a parameter, all subsequent parameters must also have default values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the function signature 'void foo(int x, int y = 10);', which call is valid?
m_Item1.Explanation: The call 'foo(5);' is valid because it uses the default value for 'y'. 'foo(5, 3);' is also valid, but it's not presented as an option.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using default parameters in a header file, where should the default value be specified?

Options.OptionText: In the function declaration inside the header file.
In the function definition inside the source file.
Both A and B are correct.
Default parameters cannot be used in header files.

m_Item1.Explanation: The default value must be specified in the function declaration in the header file, not in the definition in the source file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a scenario where default parameters are useful?

Options.OptionText: When a function always requires multiple parameters without exceptions.
When a function may be used with one or more standard values but allows customization if needed.
When overloading is used extensively in a program.
When a function does not take any parameters.

m_Item1.Explanation: Default parameters are useful when a function can be called with typical values but can be customized by providing specific values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about default parameters in C++ is true?

Options.OptionText: Default parameters can only be used with non-member functions.
Default parameters must be provided at the function definition in the source file.
Default parameters reduce the need for overloading similar functions.

m_Item1.Explanation: Default parameters reduce the need for multiple overloaded functions by allowing certain parameters to have common default values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider the following code: 'void print(int a, int b = 5); int main() { print(10); }'. What will this code output?

Options.OptionText: Error: No matching function for call to 'print()'

m_Item1.Explanation: The function 'print' is called with a single argument '10', and the default value '5' is used for 'b', so it outputs '10 5'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can default parameters be used in templates?

Options.OptionText: Yes, default parameters can be used with any type of functions including templates.
No, default parameters cannot be used with templates.
Yes, but they are only allowed at the template level, not function level.
Yes, but only with function templates and not class templates.

m_Item1.Explanation: Default parameters can be used with templates, allowing parameters to have default values even within template functions and classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you provide a call to a function with more arguments than specified, assuming some parameters have default values?

Options.OptionText: The compiler ignores the extra arguments.
The additional arguments override the default values.
The extra arguments are considered as unused and discarded.

m_Item1.Explanation: Providing extra arguments overrides the default values for those parameters in the function call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a function with default parameters, how can you ensure a non-default parameter is always required by the caller?

Options.OptionText: Place it at the end of the parameter list.
Place it at the beginning of the parameter list before any default parameters.
Assign it a default value of 0.
Declare it as a const parameter.

m_Item1.Explanation: Non-default parameters must be placed before default parameters so they are required to be explicitly provided by the caller.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a reference variable in C++?

Options.OptionText: A variable that refers to an integer value
A constant pointer to a variable

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, a reference is an alias for an already existing variable, allowing indirect access to that variable under a different name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is a reference variable initialized in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: A reference variable in C++ is initialized with the '&' operator, which is used to create an alias for another variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement about C++ references is false?

Options.OptionText: References must be initialized when declared
References can be reassigned after being initialized

m_Item1.Explanation: Unlike pointers, once a reference is initialized to an object, it cannot be changed to refer to another object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you try to create a reference to a literal directly in C++?

Options.OptionText: Creates a copy of the literal

m_Item1.Explanation: References must be initialized with a variable, not with a literal value, leading to a compile-time error if attempted otherwise.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following syntax is correct for declaring a reference variable?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax for declaring a reference variable is using the '&' symbol followed by the variable name to assign it to a previously declared variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of reference variables in C++?

Options.OptionText: To provide aliases for existing variables

m_Item1.Explanation: Reference variables provide an alias or alternative name to access an existing variable, offering a way to indirectly modify the variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a reference variable in C++ refer to a null value?
m_Item1.Explanation: References in C++ cannot be null. They must be initialized to refer to a valid object or variable since they are not separate objects like pointers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is one key difference between a pointer and a reference in C++?

Options.OptionText: Pointers are aliases; references are addresses
References must be initialized; pointers can be uninitialized
Pointers can be reassigned; references cannot
References can be incremented; pointers cannot

m_Item1.Explanation: One key difference is that pointers can be reassigned to different addresses during their lifetime, whereas a reference must remain bound to the object it initially refers to.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why can't you change what a reference refers to after its initialization?

Options.OptionText: Because they point to memory addresses
Due to their nature as constant pointers
Since they are not actual variables themselves
Because they're tied to an object's lifecycle

m_Item1.Explanation: References are not separate memory objects; they are just aliases. Thus, the reference itself can't be changed to refer to another object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which scenario would you use a reference variable instead of a pointer?

Options.OptionText: When you need a modifiable, re-assignable address
If you require an alias without null-checking
If you want to store the variable's address in a different variable

m_Item1.Explanation: Use references when you need an alias that doesn't require null-checking or re-assignment, providing a safer option than pointers in some scenarios.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'new' operator in C++?

Options.OptionText: To create a copy of an existing object
To allocate memory dynamically for a variable

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'new' operator in C++ is used to allocate memory dynamically on the heap for a variable or object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about the 'delete' operator?

Options.OptionText: It allocates memory for an array
It frees the memory allocated by the 'new' operator
It changes a variable's data type

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'delete' operator in C++ is used to free the memory that was previously allocated using the 'new' operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you use 'delete' on a pointer that points to a local variable?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using 'delete' on a pointer that points to a local variable leads to undefined behavior because such memory is not allocated by 'new'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you properly deallocate an array of integers allocated using 'new'?
m_Item1.Explanation: To deallocate memory allocated for an array using 'new', you should use 'delete[]' followed by the pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is memory leak in the context of using 'new' and 'delete'?

Options.OptionText: When memory is allocated using 'new' but not released using 'delete'.
When a program crashes due to stack overflow

m_Item1.Explanation: Memory leak occurs when memory is allocated using 'new' but not released when it is no longer needed, leading to wastage of resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly initializes an integer pointer with memory allocated by 'new'?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option B correctly initializes an integer pointer with memory allocated dynamically using 'new'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the return value of 'new' if it fails to allocate memory?
m_Item1.Explanation: If 'new' fails to allocate the requested memory, it throws a std::bad_alloc exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which C++ feature largely eliminates the need to explicitly use 'new' and 'delete'?
m_Item1.Explanation: Smart pointers in C++, like std::unique_ptr and std::shared_ptr, can manage memory automatically, largely eliminating the need to manually use 'new' and 'delete'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If 'new' successfully allocates memory but the constructor throws an exception, what will happen?

Options.OptionText: The allocated memory is automatically released

m_Item1.Explanation: If 'new' allocates memory but the constructor throws an exception, the memory is automatically deallocated, preventing memory leaks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a correct statement to delete a pointer to an integer allocated by 'new'?
m_Item1.Explanation: Option B is the correct statement to delete a pointer to an integer allocated by 'new'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is correct about this pointer in C++?

Options.OptionText: this pointer is passed as a hidden argument in all the functions of a class
this pointer is passed as a hidden argument in all non-static functions of a class
this pointer is passed as a hidden argument in all static functions of a class
this pointer is passed as a hidden argument in all static variables of a class

m_Item1.Explanation: this pointer is passed as a hidden argument in all non static variables of a class

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is std in C++?

Options.OptionText: std is a standard class in C++
std is a standard namespace in C++
std is a standard header file in C++
std is a standard file reading header in C++

m_Item1.Explanation: std is a standard namespace present in C++ which contains different stream classes and objects like cin, cout, etc. and other standard functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
int main(int argc, char const *argv[])
{
    cout<<"Hello World";
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: cout is defined under the namespace std and without including std namespace we cannot cout, therefore, the program gives an error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int x = 1;
int main()
{
    int x = 2;
    {
        int x = 3;
        cout << ::x << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: While printing x we are using :: operator hence the refernce is given to global variable hence the global variable x = 1 is printed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
class A
{
  ~A(){
    cout<<"Destructor called ";
  }
};
int main()
{
    A a;
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Whenever a destructor is private then one should not define any normal object as it will be destroyed at the end of the program which will call destructor and as destructor is private the program gives error during compile while in case of pointer object the compiler at compile does not know about the object, therefore, does not gives compile error. Hence when the destructor is private then the programmer can declare pointer object but cannot declare a normal object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the delete operator in C++?

Options.OptionText: To remove elements from a container.
To deallocate memory for an object or array allocated with new.
To remove a function from a class.
To delete a file from the file system.

m_Item1.Explanation: To deallocate memory for an object or array allocated with new.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between delete and delete[] in C++?

Options.OptionText: delete is syntactically correct but delete[] is wrong and hence will give an error if used in any case
delete is used to delete normal objects whereas delete[] is used to pointer objects
delete is a keyword whereas delete[] is an identifier
delete is used to delete single object whereas delete[] is used to multiple(array/pointer of) objects

m_Item1.Explanation: Explanation: delete is used to delete a single object initiated using new keyword whereas delete[] is used to delete a group of objects initiated with the new operator.
